issues.claim: yoga is skillfulness in action — not effort, not enthusiasm, not vague mindful presence
issues.objection: Lexically clean: kauśalam = skill/expertise, anchored on Shankara's gloss ('the attitude of the skilful, the wisdom of equanimity with regard to success and failure while engaged in actions') and Ramanuja's ('evenness of mind during action, possible through great skill'). The engineering body explicitly preserves the Sanskrit term kauśalam and forecloses the loose 'effort/passion/flow' readings. One framing nit: the Prabhupada/Gaudiya tradition reads 'yoga' here as 'Kṛṣṇa consciousness' (the synonyms field of vedabase records 'yogaḥ — Kṛṣṇa consciousness'). The engineering layer simplifies toward the Shankara/Ramanuja convergence (skill = samatvam-during-action) which is the doctrinal floor; the theistic specificity of the Gaudiya reading is recorded in the source pack rather than in the engineering body. Acceptable, but a careful Sanskritist notes that 'yoga' in this verse for one tradition means specifically devotional service yoked to the Lord.

notes: The engineering layer correctly preserves: (a) the verse's two-claim structure — 'one joined to buddhi sheds BOTH good and bad' (ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte) and 'yoga is skillfulness in action' (yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam); (b) the surprising claim that praise-attached action binds as much as fear-driven action — directly from Shankara's reading that BOTH sukṛta and duṣkṛte must be shed; (c) the Sanskrit terminology (kauśalam, samatvam, buddhi-yukta, sukṛta-duṣkṛte) anchored in the engineering body. The 'skill is in the artifact, not the affect' diagnostic is exactly the Shankara-style reading. No tradition is misattributed. One minor framing note on the Gaudiya theistic-yoga reading; not critical. Pass.
